1851
Same place,
same time
London
The Great Exhibition

The interactive timeline-map and its storytelling are the result of a systematic analysis of the eighty-five assigned readings from the syllabus of the class Pratt in London: Design and Museums on Site. The material was programmatically converted to plain text and analyzed, cross-referencing the data in search of insights: names, places, years, and recurring expressions were identified, extracted, and counted across the corpus. William Morris was the most quoted name, appearing in sixteen of the readings; the V&A and the British Museum were the most cited places; and 1851 caught the attention, flagged as a key date by fourteen readings, more than any other year, with fifty-two mentions in all. Nineteen of the thirty figures on the map are named at least once in the readings.

The insights from this analysis set the topic that conducts the narrative: a photograph of one time and one place, and everything that was happening inside it — narratives that occasionally cross, and stories with no direct connection at all. After being designed in Figma, the website was built with the support of Claude and Claude Code. Every location was verified against a primary or documentary source: the 1851 census, English Heritage blue plaques, diaries, or letters. Figures without a confirmable London address in 1851 appear as visitors at documented locations or were left off entirely. No approximate addresses were accepted.

The final product is a single HTML file (plus the Leaflet library and map tiles, loaded at runtime), published with GitHub Pages. The map runs on Leaflet, with a custom base style built in Mapbox Studio on OpenStreetMap data. The timeline is drawn as SVG from the same dataset, and the portraits are period images sourced from Wikimedia Commons and embedded in the file. The AI assisted with corpus analysis, research retrieval, drafting, and code. The topic, the argument, the design, and the writing are the author's.

Alice Viggiani
June 2026

Project created as a final assignment for the class Pratt in London: Design and Museums on Site, taught by Professor Sarah Lichtman.

Base map © Mapbox © OpenStreetMap, custom style by the author
Pictures via Wikimedia

Same place,
same time

London
1851

Who was where
at the year
of the Great
Exhibition

1851 is a remarkable year for the industrial age, triggered by the Great Exhibition that took place in London, at the Crystal Palace.

This scrollytelling story connects the dots between the prominent figures who were coexisting in such an effervescent city in that remarkable year: 1851 London.

Pratt in London
June 2026
Alice Viggiani

↓
The argument

There was a time, in one city, when a handful of people who had made or would make history lived close by, mostly without noticing one another. They might cross paths, though, at the most popular event of the age. In 1851, London was the stage for the Great Exhibition of the Works of Industry of All Nations, held in Hyde Park inside a prefabricated iron-and-glass building raised in about nine months especially for the fair. And each of the thirty figures gathered here made a strong statement about it.

What follows is a story about the effervescent scene unfolding in London in this particular year of 1851, and the main characters somehow acting in it, each making their own statement about it. Taking the Great Exhibition and the Crystal Palace, where this remarkable event took place, as a representative starting point for the optimism of the industrial age, the story unfolds the thoughts, actions, writings, and even denials of thirty prominent figures toward a modernization process that was widely debated at the time.

To create this visual narrative, a corpus of eighty-five readings from a course syllabus served as the theoretical basis and source of insights. With the help of an LLM, the most quoted names, places, expressions, and periods were counted. By a clear margin, 1851 was the most cited year in the corpus: fourteen of the eighty-five readings flag it as a key date — no other year comes close. The Great Exhibition itself is named in eleven readings, twenty-eight times in all. The analysis revealed a common debate running through radically different figures in several fields of knowledge: artists, designers and architects, philosophers, writers, politicians, and scientists.

The industrial era,
and the statements about it

In that sense, the event and the period offered a perfect context to raise this discussion and to illustrate so many ideas about the industrial revolution and its reflections in the worlds of art and science. In fact, more than six million people passed through the Great Exhibition before it closed in October. The Exhibition has since become the standard shorthand for Victorian industrial confidence, a milestone in the optimism of the industrial era. Historical figures whose visits to the fair are documented, such as Charles Darwin, Karl Marx, Lewis Carroll, and Charles Dickens, were added to the narrative to represent the event's broad reach. All thirty figures on the map were verified in London during 1851 through census records, blue plaques, diaries, or letters.

On the Origin of Species was taking shape in the same streets where Frankenstein had been written, close to the reading-room research that would become Capital. Yet the canon does not register the novelist editing a radical journal above the Strand, the political exile walking daily from Soho to the British Museum, the fugitives from a Georgia plantation staging a protest on the Exhibition floor, or the painter dying under a false name in Chelsea. This piece gathers that broth of culture and knowledge, usually studied apart, and reunites it on a single map: where each figure lived, worked, or simply visited that year, and, above all, what they were thinking at the time.

1

The Crystal Palace, the fair, and its believers

The story begins where the confidence was loudest: inside and around the Crystal Palace, with the people who commissioned it, engineered it, colored it, and, in two remarkable cases, turned its own floor against it.

Queen Victoria

Monarchthe crown

Victoria opened the Exhibition before a crowd of twenty-five thousand and returned to the Crystal Palace some thirty-four times that summer alone. Her journal entries from opening day are among the most effusive she ever wrote.

For the crown, the Exhibition was proof that industry could be domesticated into national glory: Albert's project, Britain's shop window, an empire of manufactures arranged for a royal promenade. Hers is the official voice of industrial optimism in this story, the position every other figure on this map is either serving, qualifying, or refusing.

Henry Cole

Civil servant,
Exhibition administratorart & design

Cole organized the Exhibition as a public proof that industrial production and good design were not opposites. His Journal of Design (1849 to 1852) had already argued that the problem with industrial goods was not the machine but the absence of design principles governing it.

The Crystal Palace was his argument made physical: if you could fill a prefabricated iron-and-glass structure with the manufactured output of every nation and make it beautiful, then industry was not the enemy of culture but its vehicle. The £186,000 surplus he stewarded went on to found the South Kensington complex, which institutionalized that argument in brick and mortar.

Joseph Paxton

Architect, engineerart & design

Paxton's Crystal Palace was not just a building that housed the Exhibition; it was the Exhibition's most radical exhibit. Prefabricated in modular cast-iron components and plate glass, erected by over 2,000 workers in nine months, the structure demonstrated that industrial methods could achieve lightness, scale, and spatial drama on a previously impossible basis.

Paxton had borrowed the structural logic from his greenhouse work for the Duke of Devonshire, applying horticultural engineering to civic architecture. The building was its own argument: industry could make things that had never existed before.

Owen Jones

Architect, designerart & design

Jones was Superintendent of Works for the Exhibition and designed the Crystal Palace's interior color scheme: the iron structure painted in red, yellow, and blue primaries, derived from his study of Alhambra ornament. The Grammar of Ornament (1856), which grew directly from this work, proposed that all great decorative traditions shared underlying geometric principles that industry could learn to apply.

Where Pugin saw industrial production as irredeemable, Jones saw it as a problem of education: give manufacturers access to the right formal principles and the machine could produce well-designed goods. The Crystal Palace interior was his demonstration that industrial materials could carry sophisticated color theory.

A.W.N. Pugin

Architect, designerart & design

Pugin designed the Medieval Court inside an exhibition he despised, calling the Crystal Palace a greenhouse. His contribution was the most visited room at the show: a dense installation of Gothic metalwork, encaustic tiles, stained glass, and Catholic ornament, produced by skilled craftsmen working from medieval precedents. The contradiction is the point.

Pugin's entire theoretical position, set out in Contrasts (1836), argued that architecture and ornament were moral expressions of the society that produced them, and that industrial England had made ugly things because it had become an ugly society. He placed that argument inside the temple of industry and died the following year, partly from the effort.

Ellen and William Craft

Abolitionists, fugitives from slaverypolitics & reform

The sharpest critique of the Exhibition was not written in Soho or Denmark Hill. It was performed on the Exhibition floor. Ellen Craft had escaped a Georgia plantation in 1848 disguised as a white male planter, her husband William posing as her valet; the Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 drove them to England.

On 21 June 1851 the Crafts and William Wells Brown entered the Crystal Palace, each arm in arm with a white abolitionist of the opposite sex, and promenaded for six to seven hours through nearly every part of the building.

Their target was the American section, which displayed cotton, sugar, and tobacco with no reference to the three million enslaved people who produced them. The demonstrators paused before Hiram Powers's celebrated Greek Slave and placed beside it Punch's satirical print of a Virginia slave. The Exhibition celebrated industrial production while hiding the conditions of production; the Crafts made one of those hidden conditions walk, visibly free, through the middle of the spectacle.

Charles Darwin

Naturalistscience & engineering

Darwin's 1851 divides in two. In April his ten-year-old daughter Annie died, the loss that biographers widely treat as the end of his Christian faith. In late July he came up from Down House and spent ten days in London specifically to see the Great Exhibition.

So the man walking the aisles of industrial optimism that summer was a grieving father who had just lost his belief in providence, quietly carrying a theory of species change that would do more to unsettle Victorian certainty than anything displayed under Paxton's glass. He published two monographs on barnacles that year and said nothing about the rest. The Exhibition celebrated what industry had made of nature; Darwin was privately dismantling what everyone believed nature to be.

2

The working city

Engines, presses, and a reading room. Away from Hyde Park, the same industrial system was being extended, narrated, edited, and analyzed, at addresses within an hour's walk of each other.

Michael Faraday

Natural philosopherscience & engineering

Faraday assisted with the planning and judging of exhibits at the Exhibition, working from the Royal Institution on Albemarle Street where he had spent his entire career translating electromagnetic phenomena into the scientific language that would eventually make industrial electricity possible. In 1851 that translation was not yet complete: the Exhibition's machinery ran on steam, not electricity.

But Faraday's presence at the building links the spectacle of industrial production to its scientific substrate. The Royal Institution building is the site where the theoretical conditions for the next phase of industrialization were being worked out while the Exhibition displayed the current one.

Isambard Kingdom Brunel

Civil engineerscience & engineering

Brunel sat on the building committee for the Crystal Palace and was simultaneously constructing Paddington Station with the same contractors and the same structural logic of prefabricated iron and glass. By 1851 he had already built the Great Western Railway and the SS Great Britain, physically reorganizing England's experience of distance and time.

His presence at the Exhibition connects the building to the broader infrastructure of industrialization: the railways that brought six million visitors to Hyde Park were as much a part of the Exhibition's argument as anything inside it. The Crystal Palace did not exist in isolation; it was the terminus of a network Brunel had spent his career building.

Charles Dickens

Novelist, editorliterature

Dickens's 1851 was lived at two addresses. At Devonshire Terrace, in April, his infant daughter Dora died; in November the lease expired and the family moved to Tavistock House in Bloomsbury, where he began Bleak House before the year was out. Paxton himself had given him a tour of the Crystal Palace during construction, and Dickens's weekly Household Words covered the Exhibition repeatedly.

His response was the ambivalence of the city's great chronicler: he admired the engineering, wearied of the spectacle, and kept insisting in print that the nation congratulating itself in Hyde Park was the same nation of slums, courts, and foul air he walked through every night. The novel he started that winter opens in fog and mud, three miles and one year from the glass building everyone was celebrating.

Marian Evans (George Eliot)

Editor, writerliterature

In January 1851, Marian Evans took lodgings at the publisher John Chapman's house at 142 Strand, overlooking Somerset House and the Thames, and became the de facto editor of the Westminster Review, the engine room of radical Victorian thought. She was thirty-one, not yet a novelist, and the only woman at Chapman's evening salons, where Dickens, Herbert Spencer, and Carlyle gathered.

Her 1851 is a reminder that industrialization was also a revolution in print: steam presses, cheap periodicals, a reading public assembled at industrial scale. While the Exhibition displayed manufactured goods, the house on the Strand was manufacturing and distributing the ideas, including the scientific ones, that would define the second half of the century.

Karl Marx

Political economistpolitics & reform

Marx spent 1851 in two cramped upstairs rooms at 28 Dean Street with his wife Jenny and their children, in poverty severe enough that a Prussian police spy filed a report on the household's disorder.

Every morning he walked fifteen minutes north to the British Museum, where he began the research sessions that would take sixteen more years to become Capital. When Louis Napoleon's coup came in December, he began The Eighteenth Brumaire in these rooms, with Wilhelm Liebknecht visiting daily from three minutes away.

Six million visitors marveled at machinery and manufactures without encountering the conditions of their production. The Crystal Palace was in this reading the ideological form of industrial capitalism: it made the commodity beautiful and the worker invisible. Marx's rooms and Paxton's building stood less than an hour's walk apart, and they were utterly different versions of what 1851 meant.

3

The edges

The map's last three pins move outward, to Chelsea and the suburban hills, where the intellectual counter-arguments to the Exhibition were being written, and where one refusal came from a seventeen-year-old.

Thomas Carlyle

Historian, essayistcriticism & philosophy

Carlyle had named the enemy twenty-two years before the Crystal Palace went up: in "Signs of the Times" (1829) he called his era the Mechanical Age, one that had mechanized not only production but thought and feeling.

From the house on Cheyne Row where he had lived since 1834 and would die in 1881, he spent 1851 publishing his Life of Sterling and receiving the visitors, Dickens, Ruskin, Tennyson among them, who made the house a counter-institution to the official culture celebrating itself in Hyde Park. He was the oldest and most caustic of the Exhibition's critics, and the one the younger ones read first: Ruskin's moral economics and Morris's hatred of the cash relation both descend from this address.

John Ruskin

Art critic, authorcriticism & philosophy

Ruskin wrote the preface to The Stones of Venice volume one at his family home on Denmark Hill in February 1851, as the Crystal Palace was rising across the city. The book is the Exhibition's direct intellectual counter-argument: craft against machine, Gothic against glass and iron, moral architecture against industrial spectacle.

Its central claim, expanded two years later in "The Nature of Gothic," was that ornament records the freedom or servitude of the worker who made it, and that a society's buildings are therefore evidence of its moral condition. Ruskin dismissed Paxton's building as little more than a greenhouse of unprecedented size: a triumph of quantity with nothing to say. He finished the preface and left for Venice to research the next two volumes while the crowds queued in Hyde Park.

William Morris

Student at the time
Later, designer and poetart & design

Morris was seventeen and a student at Marlborough when he visited London with his family in 1851 and refused to enter the Exhibition on the grounds of taste. It is the founding anecdote of the Arts and Crafts movement: everything he later built, the Firm, the pattern work, the lectures collected as "The Lesser Arts," can be read as the long unpacking of that adolescent refusal.

He returned to Water House in Walthamstow at Christmas after being removed from school. The map holds him at its northeastern edge, the farthest pin from Hyde Park: in 1851 the movement that would answer the Exhibition was a teenager standing outside the door, declining to go in.

Put them back side by side and the year changes shape. Thirty lives, one square of a map, and a single vertical line that is at once a date and an address. None of them could see this picture; the simultaneity was invisible from inside it. The map holds what the year could not.

Epilogue

What the decade did with 1851

The map is a single year, but the year detonated slowly. A short aftermath, for the record:

  • 1851Turner dies in Chelsea in December, two months after the Exhibition closes.
  • 1852Pugin dies at forty. Marx publishes The Eighteenth Brumaire. Cole's Museum of Manufactures opens with objects bought from the Exhibition.
  • 1853Ruskin publishes "The Nature of Gothic." Morris goes up to Oxford.
  • 1854The Crystal Palace reopens, relocated to Sydenham. Brunel's Paddington Station opens in full.
  • 1856Owen Jones publishes The Grammar of Ornament.
  • 1857The South Kensington Museum, later the V&A, opens on land bought with the Exhibition surplus. Marian Evans begins publishing fiction as George Eliot.
  • 1859Darwin publishes On the Origin of Species. Brunel dies.
  • 1861Morris founds the Firm. The refusal of 1851 becomes a business.

Canvas is yours
Drag, zoom, click, compare

The map below is live: hold and drag it, zoom with the buttons, by double-clicking, or with cmd/ctrl + scroll. Hover any portrait to learn more, or click any name, bar, or portrait to compare lives. Every figure is colored by field, select them one by one, or by category. Switch views, recenter on the origin, 1851, Crystal Palace, or clear your selection.
